title: Alpine Wildlife
standfirst: The residents of the tundra meet on their own patient terms.

cover.alt: Bull elk with antlers in velvet standing in open alpine tundra at first light, at the Rocky Mountain National Park visitor center.

intro: Above eleven thousand feet the air gets thin and the animals get careful. Everything up there has already survived a winter, and it shows in how little they waste

images.caption: Bull elk with antlers crossing the road at the highest national park visitor center in the US

title: High Country
standfirst: Ridgelines, alpine lakes, and the long light that only shows up above eleven thousand feet.
intro: These are the places I go to be quiet. Basins that hold snow into July, lakes so still they double the sky, ridgelines that put you above everything but the weather.

images.caption: Looking up through the aspens in the afternoon
Tundra ridgeline lit by sunset along Trail Ridge Road, Rocky Mountain National Park.

title: Snowfields
standfirst: Snow, wind-scoured rock, and the long low light of the alpine winter.

cover.alt: Wind-scoured snow ridge under a deep blue winter sky, Mosquito Range.

intro: Winter above treeline empties the place out. The wildlife thins to what can survive it, the color drains to white and stone and a few hours of blue-gold light, and the wind writes and rewrites the snow into new shapes every night.
This is the season that tests whether you actually like it up there, or just like how it looks in July.

images.alt: Wind-scoured snow ridge photographed at blue hour, Mosquito Range.
images.caption: Wind-scoured ridge, blue hour — Mosquito Range.

images.alt: Snow cornice overhanging a steep couloir, Gore Range.
images.caption: Cornice over the couloir — Gore Range.

images.caption: Alpenglow on the thirteener — Sawatch Range.

images.alt: Rime ice coating stunted krummholz trees at treeline, San Juan Mountains.
images.caption: Rime ice at treeline — San Juan Mountains.

images.caption: First tracks, first light — Elk Mountains.
